title: Controlled ion-ion interactions and cavity-enhanced emission of a coherent dinuclear Eu$^{3+}$ complex
authors: Evgenij Vasilenko, Vishnu Unni Chorakkunnath, Barbora Brachnakova, Nicholas Lester Jobbitt, Senthil Kumar Kuppusamy, David Hunger, Mario Ruben
abstract: Molecular rare-earth-ion complexes offer unique opportunities for quantum technologies by combining the intrinsic coherence properties of rare-earth ions with chemically tunable molecular environments. A crucial capability is the realization of multi-qubit architectures with defined qubit couplings to enable two-qubit quantum gates. Here, we investigate the optical coherence properties and excitation-induced interactions of two Eu$^{3+}$-based molecular complexes, comparing a mononuclear reference system with a dinuclear analogue in which two Eu$^{3+}$ ions are positioned at a well-defined intramolecular distance of about 7 Angstrom. Using cryogenic ensemble spectroscopy, including spectral hole burning, free-induction decay, and photon echo measurements at temperatures down to 100 mK, we demonstrate long optical coherence times $T_{2,\text{o}}$ of up to 9 $μ$s. As a key step toward scalable multi-qubit architectures, a control-target sequence was implemented to probe conditional ion-ion interactions, revealing a stronger interaction-induced dephasing in the dinuclear complex. Finally, we show the integration of the dinuclear complex into a fiber-based optical microcavity, and observe an 380-fold emission enhancement of the $\mathrm{}^5\mathrm{D}_0\rightarrow\mathrm{}^7\mathrm{F}_0$ transition. Together, these results position molecular rare-earth complexes as versatile and chemically tunable building blocks for scalable quantum technologies.
summary: The research investigates $	ext{Eu}^{3+}$ molecular complexes to create robust platforms for quantum information processing. By synthesizing and spectroscopically characterizing mononuclear and dinuclear variants, the authors demonstrated long coherence times and controlled ion interactions. Crucially, they enhanced emission by coupling the complex into an optical microcavity, proving its viability as a tunable solid-state qubit material.
main_problem: The research aims to develop molecular rare-earth ion complexes as chemically tunable, scalable building blocks for multi-qubit quantum architectures.
main_result: The study demonstrated long optical coherence times ($T_{2,	ext{o}}$ up to $9 \mu	ext{s}$) and achieved a significant 380-fold emission enhancement by integrating the dinuclear complex into an optical microcavity.
method: A combination of advanced cryogenic ensemble spectroscopy (SHB, Photon Echo) was used to measure coherence times and ion interactions, complemented by cavity QED measurements for Purcell enhancement.
model_or_system: The system involves mononuclear and dinuclear $	ext{Eu}^{3+}$ molecular complexes. The study models the interaction between these ions using dipole-dipole coupling and probes quantum coherence via time-domain spectroscopy techniques.
key_observables: Optical coherence times ($T_{2,	ext{o}}$), inhomogeneous linewidth ($\Gamma_{	ext{inh}}$), emission enhancement factor (Purcell factor $	ext{F}_{	ext{eff}}	ext{P}$), and interaction-induced dephasing rates ($\Gamma_c$).
important_parameters: Temperatures down to $100 	ext{ mK}$, defined ion separations ($\sim 7 	ext{ \AA}$), and cavity quality factors ($Q = 3 	imes 10^5$).
main_assumptions: The molecular structure allows for deterministic control over ion separation, enabling the realization of controlled two-qubit interactions.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate PL spectra showing enhanced transitions in the dinuclear complex and time-domain measurements (FID/Echo) demonstrating coherence decay influenced by external fields or cavity coupling.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from structural characterization and synthesis to measuring fundamental optical properties ($	ext{T}_{2,	ext{o}}$, linewidths), then investigating controlled ion-ion interactions, and finally demonstrating the enhancement of emission via microcavity integration.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the challenge of creating solid-state quantum emitters with controllable coupling strengths by using molecular chemistry to engineer inter-ion distances, which is central to building scalable quantum processors in AMO physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Photoluminescence properties of the mononuclear and dinuclear Eu3+ complexes. (A): Molecular structures of Eu-mono (left) and Eu-di (right). (B): Energy-level scheme of Eu3+ showing the relevant radiative decay channels from the 5D0 excited state into the 7FJ manifold as observed in the emission spectra. (C): Room-temperature photoluminescence spectra of Eu-mono (upper panel) and Eu-di (lower panel). The dinuclear complex shows a significantly higher branching ratio of the coherent 5D0 → 7F0 transition, which is highlighted in red in the spectra. (D): Photolu- minescence excitation spectra recorded at about 100 mK, revealing the inhomogeneous broadening of the 5D0 → 7F0...

Figure 2: Spectral hole burning and hyperfine state lifetime. (A): Absorption-based spectral hole burning mea- surements for both complexes, shown with a vertical offset for clarity. Lorentzian fits yield full width at half maximum (FWHM) linewidths of 0.42(2) MHz for Eu-mono and 0.47(3) MHz for Eu-di. (B): Hole depth evolution for both com- plexes. Eu-mono (upper panel) exhibits a single-exponential decay with a relaxation time of 4.3(6) s, and no further decay within the 500 s measurement window, suggesting hour-long spin lifetime. Eu-di (lower panel) shows a bi-exponential decay with time constants of 2.7(12) s and 180(30) s.

Figure 3: Optical coherence measurements. (A): Optical free-induction decay measurements obtained using a π/2 pulse followed by a heterodyne readout pulse. The two traces are vertically offset for clarity and yield optical dephasing times of T ∗ 2,o(FID) = 530(20) ns for Eu-mono and T ∗ 2,o(FID) = 790(40) ns for Eu-di. (B): Two-pulse photon echo decays recorded at different excitation powers, with three power levels shown in each panel. Increasing optical power acceler- ates the echo decay and reduces the optical coherence time T2,o. The longest observed echo decays are approximately 9 µs for Eu-mono and 4 µs for Eu-di. (C): Effective homogeneous linewidth Γeff as a function of excitation...

Figure 4: Probing ion-ion interactions via controlled excitation of a sub-ensemble. (A): Pulse sequence used to probe ion-ion interactions (top panel) and schematic illustration of the control-target ion configuration (bottom panel). A frequency-selective control pulse (red pulse) is applied to a subset of ions at frequency ωc, while the optical coherence of spectrally distinct target ions at ωt is monitored using a two-pulse photon echo sequence (orange pulses). The control and target ions are represented as effective three-level systems with ground states |0⟩, |1⟩and optically excited state |e⟩. The interaction between control and target ions is described by the dipole–dipole coupling...

Figure 5: Purcell enhancement of the coherent transition. (A): Schematic illustration of the fiber-based micro- cavity setup employed in this work. (B): Fitted cavity resonance in the presence of the thin-film doped with Eu-di. The second polarization mode visible at a frequency detuning of 2.4 GHz arises from the slight asymmetry in the concave pro- file of the fiber. (C): Purcell enhancement of the coherent 5D0 → 7F0 transition at room temperature (blue), and at 6 K (green).
